nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; found at depths of roughly 4–30 m along rocky and coral reefs

diet.notes: Has been documented consuming sponges, sessile invertebrates, marine algae, and zooplankton in the wild. In aquariums, has been reported to accept spirulina, marine algae, high-quality angelfish preparations containing sponge, mysis shrimp, and other meaty foods. Feeding small portions two to three times daily is generally advised.

summary: A large, striking East Pacific angelfish reaching up to 36 cm; generally considered not reef-safe and best suited to experienced keepers with very large FOWLR systems of 1000 L or more.
careNotes:
The Passer Angelfish (also known as the King Angelfish) is native to the Eastern Pacific, ranging from the Gulf of California south to Peru and including the Galapagos Islands. Adults tend to develop a bold dark-blue coloration with a distinctive white vertical band, yellow-edged dorsal fin, and yellow caudal and pectoral fins. Juveniles are visually quite distinct, displaying orange and white/blue striping, and have been documented performing cleaning behavior on other fish.

This species has been reported to grow to around 35–36 cm TL in the wild, making it one of the larger marines angelfishes kept in the hobby. A minimum tank of 1000 L (approximately 250–265 US gallons) is generally advised, with many specialist sources recommending 500+ US gallons for long-term adult housing; the fish is an active swimmer that benefits from ample open water. Tank length is considered more important than volume alone.

In the wild, Holacanthus passer tends to form monogamous pairs but does not defend exclusive territories. In captivity it has been known to be highly aggressive toward conspecifics and other angelfish species, and is generally best kept as the sole angelfish in a system unless the tank is exceptionally large. It tends to be semi-aggressive toward other large fish and may intimidate passive or smaller tankmates.

The species is generally considered not reef-safe: it has been documented grazing on live rock and has been known to nip at stony and soft corals, clam mantles, and other sessile invertebrates. It is well suited to a large FOWLR (fish-only-with-live-rock) setup.

Diet should include a variety of high-quality foods: sponge-based angelfish preparations, marine algae, spirulina, and meaty items such as mysis shrimp, krill, and similar. Feeding two to three times daily in small portions is generally advised.

Care level is considered advanced given the large tank requirements, the species' sensitivity to water quality fluctuations, and its dietary needs for sponge-based foods. Some sources rate it as moderate, reflecting that healthy individuals are relatively hardy once established.

Sources disagree modestly on temperature (range reported: 22–29 °C across sources) and on tank size (500 L to ~1900 L); the values recorded here represent an averaged and cautiously biased estimate. Lifespan estimates range from 10 to 20 years across sources.

Protogynous hermaphroditism has been reported in related Holacanthus species; field data on H. passer specifically is limited. The species is oviparous and pelagic spawning has been documented in the wild (April–November, peaking October–November at sunset). Captive breeding has not been reliably reported.
breedingNotes: Spawning in the wild has been documented from April through November, reportedly peaking around October–November near sunset. The species is a pelagic spawner, releasing eggs and sperm into the water column. Captive breeding has not been reliably reported; successful reproduction in a home aquarium is considered essentially impractical given the tank sizes required and the species' behavior.

additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (35.6 cm TL confirmed), native range (Gulf of California to Peru including Galapagos), temperature (22.9–29.1 °C), taxonomy (family Pomacanthidae, order Acanthuriformes), and reproductive behavior.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (14 in / 35.6 cm), temperature (72–78 °F), salinity (1.020–1.025), care level (moderate), native range, lifespan (15–20 years), and breeding seasonality.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (35 cm), temperature (24–28 °C), salinity (SG 1.023–1.026), pH (8.0–8.4), and minimum tank size (500 L stated, though considered conservative for adults). Care level listed as moderate to difficult.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic spawner; captive breeding has not been reliably achieved. Wild spawning occurs April–November.
